guideline_references: CDC Toxic Shock Syndrome (Staphylococcal) 2011 Case Definition
Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia): Sepsis and septic shock; toxic shock syndrome
intentional_test_features: Red-herring rash: the diffuse macular erythema is described by the patient as a 'sunburn', which invites anchoring on a benign cause or a simple viral exanthem — the test is whether the system weights the accompanying shock, high fever and altered mental status as a toxin-mediated systemic emergency rather than a skin problem.

Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present after-hours clinician who measured shock vitals, examined mucosae, removed the retained tampon and called a Priority 1 ambulance; the AI Doctor cannot take vitals or examine, and is scored on recognising distributive shock, escalating to 000, and instructing the patient/carer in the source-control and positioning steps they can actually perform.

Menstrual/tampon source control: the actionable, patient-performable intervention (immediate removal of a retained high-absorbency tampon) must be elicited and instructed sensitively — it is a rare example of a definitive source-control step a telehealth patient can do themselves.

Differential discrimination: measles/viral exanthem, anaphylaxis and meningococcaemia are the classic mimics; each is distinguished by the rash morphology and the presence of profound distributive shock.
Age inferred (young menstruating adult) and sex inferred — flagged.
Source category: AUC (Acute Urgent Care), source note AUC-050.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
